title: VPN gateway with weak IKE crypto + pre-shared key leak
narrative: A Site-to-Site VPN gateway accepts IKEv1 with DES/3DES + weak PSK. An attacker on-path captures IKE packets and offline-cracks the PSK, then establishes their own tunnel to pivot into the VNet from anywhere on the internet.

steps.action: Capture IKE session; offline-crack the PSK using GPU cluster.
Establish attacker-side tunnel; appear as internal node in Azure VNet.

title: Private DNS zone hijack via delegation
narrative: Private DNS zones are linked to VNets without strict IAM. An attacker with Private DNS Zone Contributor creates a CNAME for an internal service name to attacker-controlled storage, harvesting internal traffic intended for legitimate endpoints.

steps.action: Add CNAME internal-api -> attacker.blob.core.windows.net.
Resolves internal-api to attacker storage; sends auth tokens in request headers.

title: ExpressRoute without MACsec + carrier compromise
narrative: ExpressRoute circuit does not enable MAC Layer Security. Traffic between corporate edge and Azure travels clear through the carrier's network. A carrier-level adversary (or compromised physical cross-connect) can sniff or inject.

steps.action: Tap the ExpressRoute path at the MMR.
Harvest session tokens; inject forged responses.

title: NAT gateway with outbound any + data exfil channel
narrative: NAT gateway allows outbound to any destination and the VNet has no egress monitoring. A compromised VM can exfiltrate to attacker DNS or HTTP servers without triggering any network alert.

steps.action: DNS-tunnel data to attacker.com via NAT gateway.
Reassemble tunnelled data at C2 server.

services_at_risk: Any private resource the VM can read

title: Cross-tenant VNet peering without RBAC scoping
narrative: A VNet peering is established with a partner tenant and both ends allow 'Allow forwarded traffic' without careful firewall rules. Compromise of a partner resource yields Layer-3 reachability to your private subnets.

steps.action: Compromise a partner VNet VM.
Route through peering into home VNet.

title: Service endpoint bypass via compromised subnet
narrative: A storage account is protected by service endpoint restrictions listing trusted subnets. Any compromised VM in those subnets can read the storage account with no further auth. Service endpoints authenticate subnets, not users.

steps.action: Call storage REST using the VM's managed identity.
Read blobs; firewall treats request as legitimate.

services_at_risk: Any resource protected by service endpoint

title: Application Gateway with SSL passthrough + backend TLS off
narrative: App Gateway terminates TLS and sends plain HTTP to backend pools over the VNet. Any VNet-scoped adversary can sniff the internal traffic or inject responses.

steps.action: Sniff backend HTTP traffic.
Replay captured session cookies.

title: Front Door without WAF + origin direct-exposure
narrative: Front Door WAF is disabled and the origin (App Service or VM) also accepts direct internet traffic. Attackers discover the origin IP and attack it directly, bypassing any Front Door protection entirely.

steps.action: DNS history lookup or certificate-transparency search for the origin IP.
Attack origin directly; WAF never sees the traffic.

title: CDN caching a secret-bearing response
narrative: A CDN rule caches any GET response including responses that inadvertently carry Authorization headers in Location redirects, cookies in Set-Cookie, or secrets in HTML. Cache keys match only URL; many users share the 'same' cache entry.

steps.action: Response has Set-Cookie with auth token + no Vary: Cookie header.
steps.gain: Token baked into CDN cache entry.

steps.action: Requests same URL; CDN serves cached response with another user's token.

services_at_risk: Any app fronted by misconfigured CDN

title: Private Link DNS misconfig forces fallback to public
narrative: A storage account has Private Link but the private DNS zone is not linked to the consuming VNet. DNS falls back to the public endpoint; traffic now traverses the public internet instead of the private channel. The admin thinks it's private; it isn't.

steps.action: VNet resolves *.blob.core.windows.net to the public IP.
Sniff the assumed-private traffic.

services_at_risk: Any Private Link consumer with broken DNS

title: NSG priority collision allows unintended traffic
narrative: Two NSG rules target the same port but with different access decisions; the lower-priority Allow comes first due to rule-number ordering. The admin believes a Deny is in effect but the Allow fires. The visible effect is an 'open' port that shouldn't be.

steps.action: Port-scan discovers 'blocked' port is actually open.
Exploit the reachable service.

title: Subnet without NSG and with a publicly-reachable VM
narrative: A subnet has no NSG attached and contains a VM with a public IP. Azure network security boils down to 'the VM's OS firewall', which is often Windows Firewall exclusions or permissive Linux iptables. Any service the VM runs is internet-reachable.

steps.action: Port-scan the VM's public IP; find an exposed service.
Exploit the service or brute-force its auth.

services_at_risk: The VM + anything its MI can reach

title: DDoS protection disabled on internet-facing resources
narrative: VNets hosting internet-facing apps do not have DDoS Protection Standard. A volumetric attack takes the app offline; the business lives with rate-limit downtime plus reputational damage.

steps.action: Launch volumetric UDP flood on public IP.
Sustain attack; basic DDoS mitigation (per-IP throttle) cannot absorb.

title: VNet flow log missing during incident
narrative: VNet NSG flow logs are not enabled. During an incident, SOC cannot see which internal IPs talked to compromised VMs. Reconstructing the lateral movement path requires host-based forensics only.

steps.action: Lateral-move across VNet without leaving flow record.
Cannot enumerate affected hosts during IR.

title: Bastion hairpin into peer subnet
narrative: Azure Bastion is deployed in a hub VNet that peers to many spoke VNets. Any authenticated Bastion user can SSH into any VM in any peered subnet. No subnet-level scoping applied — so a developer with read RBAC can reach prod VMs through Bastion.

steps.action: Open Bastion; connect to a VM in a prod subnet they shouldn't.
Pivot to further prod systems via Bastion.

data_at_risk: Any VM reachable via hub Bastion
